---
title: "Hints"
format: html
---

Hints are organised by exercise.
Try to solve each exercise before looking here.
If you are stuck, discuss with your partner first, then check the relevant section.

---

## Exercise 1: Wide to long

- Use `starts_with("item_")` inside `cols` to select all item columns at once.
- `names_to` defines the name of the new column that will hold the item names.
- `values_to` defines the name of the new column that will hold the responses.

---

## Exercise 2: Plot the data

- Before plotting, summarise to one proportion correct value per participant using `mean(correct)`, grouped by `c(pid, group)`.
- Pipe the summarised data directly into `ggplot()`.
- Use `geom_violin()` for the distribution shape and `geom_jitter()` for individual data points.

---

## Exercise 3: Single-level model

- The outcome variable goes on the left of `~`, the predictors on the right.
- You have two predictors: `group` and `pre_score_z`.
- `family = binomial` is already provided — this tells `glm()` to fit a logistic regression.

---

## Exercise 4: Multilevel model

- The fixed effects structure is the same as Exercise 3.
- Random effects are added inside parentheses: `(1 | grouping_variable)`.
- Think about what the grouping variable is — what are observations nested within?

---

## Extension exercises

---

## Exercise 5: Wide to long (full data)

- The pivot works exactly as in Exercise 1.
- The additional `test_time` column does not need to be pivoted — it stays as-is.

---

## Exercise 6: Plot the data (full data)

- Summarise by `c(pid, group, test_time)` to get one value per person per timepoint.
- Use `facet_wrap(~ variable)` to split the plot into panels by a variable.
- The geoms are the same as Exercise 2.

---

## Exercise 7: Single-level model (full data)

- You have three predictors: `group`, `test_time`, and `pre_score_z`.
- Think about which predictors should interact with each other and which should not.
- Use `*` between two predictors to fit their main effects and interaction in one step.

---

## Exercise 8: Multilevel model (full data)

- The fixed effects structure is the same as Exercise 7.
- A varying slope for `test_time` is specified as `(1 + test_time | pid)`.
- Think about what a varying slope means: each participant has their own trajectory over time.
